title: When the ability to smell goes away
gist: Covid brought unprecedented research attention to smell loss, revealing that olfaction is tightly coupled to brain health: anosmia can be an early biomarker of Parkinson's, Alzheimer's and other neurodegenerative disease, while cheap olfactory training helps roughly a third of patients partially recover.

themes.theme: Prevalence and clinical neglect of smell disorders

insights.claim: Researchers estimate up to 22 percent of the population lives with some smell impairment such as hyposmia (partial loss) or anosmia (complete loss).
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: A one-in-five prevalence implies a large, historically ignored addressable market for diagnostics and therapeutics targeting olfactory dysfunction.
insights.locator: Researchers estimate that up to 22 percent

insights.claim: Smell disorders have historically been poorly understood, underdiagnosed, and minimized by clinicians, who often told patients to simply learn to live with the loss.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: Chronic under-diagnosis signals white-space for standardized smell testing and specialist referral pathways.
insights.locator: Yet the conditions have been poorly understood

insights.claim: 19th-century French brain researcher Paul Broca labeled olfaction the 'bestial sense' and argued humans traded smell for intellect, a view that led to decades of scientific neglect but has since been disproven.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: Explains why the field is nascent and under-researched relative to vision and hearing, leaving room for fast discovery.
insights.quote: “Humans are actually quite good at smelling,”

insights.claim: The WHO reports 780 million confirmed Covid-19 cases since December 2019, and one 2023 Laryngoscope survey found 60 percent of infected individuals experienced smell loss, most temporarily.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: A simultaneous mass-scale natural experiment in smell loss is what funded and accelerated the entire olfaction research pipeline.
insights.locator: There have been 780 million reported cases

insights.claim: Covid causing millions of noses to malfunction at once spurred newfound appreciation and research funding for a previously undervalued sense.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: The research momentum is pandemic-driven and may fade with funding cycles; watch which labs sustain output.
insights.locator: With Covid causing millions of noses

insights.claim: Unlike vision or hearing, smell signals bypass the thalamus and travel directly into the limbic system, wiring olfaction straight to the amygdala (emotion) and hippocampus (memory).
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: This direct anatomical link is the mechanistic basis for using smell as a window into cognitive and psychiatric conditions.
insights.locator: We now know that olfaction initially bypasses the thalamus

insights.claim: The olfactory bulbs are among the few adult brain regions that generate new neurons, but are also the most vulnerable entry point for viruses, bacteria, toxins, and possibly microplastics.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: Adult neurogenesis in the bulbs is the biological rationale for training-based recovery and neuroplasticity therapies.
insights.locator: Furthermore, the olfactory bulbs are now known

insights.claim: In the early 1990s Linda Buck and Richard Axel discovered a family of roughly 1,000 mammalian genes producing an equal number of olfactory receptors, work that won a 2004 Nobel Prize and launched modern smell research.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: The receptor-gene family is a large druggable target space and the foundation for any receptor-level therapeutics.
insights.locator: In the early 1990s, Linda Buck and Richard Axel

insights.claim: Humans can perceive millions and perhaps a trillion distinct odors, yet scientists still have not cracked how the brain converts airborne molecules into perception.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: The unsolved coding problem is the frontier where digital-scent and diagnostic breakthroughs would originate.
insights.quote: “But we still haven’t cracked the code of how the brain turns them into perception.”

insights.claim: Odor input from the two nostrils is maintained as separate channels rather than merging, letting the brain track which nostril detected a smell to help localize its source in space.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: A non-obvious spatial-encoding mechanism relevant to sensory prosthetics and scent-based navigation research.

themes.theme: Smell loss as early neurodegeneration biomarker

insights.claim: Toxic proteins that damage the substantia nigra in Parkinson's may first accumulate in the olfactory bulbs, making smell loss one of the earliest, prodromal signs years before motor symptoms appear.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: If validated, cheap smell testing could enable pre-symptomatic Parkinson's screening, a major early-detection market.
insights.locator: A defining feature of the disorder

insights.claim: Loss of smell is also an early sign of Alzheimer's and Lewy body dementia, and altered olfactory ability appears in depression, schizophrenia, and autism.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: Smell as a cross-cutting CNS readout points to a broad diagnostic platform spanning neurology and psychiatry.
insights.locator: Loss of smell also can be an early sign

insights.claim: Researchers have associated smell disorders with an astounding 139 neurological, physical, and hereditary conditions, from alcoholism to Zika/Guillain-Barre syndrome.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: The breadth of associations argues for smell testing as a cheap, high-signal general health biomarker.
insights.locator: In fact, researchers have associated smell disorders

insights.claim: Across every cause of smell loss studied, about 30 percent of patients improve after months of olfactory training, rising to as much as 50 percent when paired with a steroid sinus rinse.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: A simple, inexpensive intervention with 30-50 percent response is a low-cost baseline any drug therapy must beat.

insights.claim: In the first US randomized controlled trial of olfactory training, six of 19 experimental patients showed significant improvement versus only two of 16 controls over six months.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: Modest but real RCT effect sizes set the evidentiary bar and quantify the unmet need for better therapies.
insights.locator: Only patients with loss of smell for more than a year

insights.claim: A 2024 meta-analysis of 36 olfactory training studies found statistically significant improvement across all olfactory domains, with individual studies reporting 11 to 68 percent gains after 12 to 16 weeks.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: Meta-analytic confirmation de-risks olfactory training as a validated protocol worth productizing at scale.
insights.locator: A 2024 meta-analysis of 36 olfactory training studies

insights.claim: In adults 55 and older, olfactory training was associated with improved semantic verbal fluency and working memory, reduced depressive symptoms, and protection from cognitive decline.
insights.speaker: Knowable Magazine
insights.so_what: If confirmed, smell training becomes a candidate low-cost intervention against age-related cognitive decline and depression.
insights.locator: There’s also early evidence that olfactory training
